query: clinical guidelines for occlusal equilibration reduction targeting upper buccal and lower lingual cusps

retrieved_docs.title: Stability in Mandibular Denture -- Issues of Concern -- A. Occlusal Surface
retrieved_docs.content: In the monoplane occlusal scheme, positioning zero degrees (cuspless/flat) teeth slightly lingual to the mandibular ridge crest enhances the denture stability. The lingualized occlusion limits the range of excursive balance and direct forces to the lingual side of the mandibular ridge during working side contacts. These contacts may reduce the horizontal stresses, thereby enhancing denture stability by controlling the leverages induced by lateral/protrusive (eccentric) tooth contacts.

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Buccal/Lingual Reduction
retrieved_docs.content: Reducing the buccal and lingual surfaces is the third step in the preparation. The reduction of the buccal and lingual mucosa is optional. Some authors suggested preparing the buccal and lingual walls to create a gingivally inclined long bevel to make crown placement easier. On the other hand, others advocated for none to minimal (0.5 to 1 mm) preparation on the vestibular and lingual aspect of the crown, unless pronounced enamel convexity should only be reduced by a small amount. [12] Duggal and Curzon recommended trying the selected crown before lingual and buccal reduction. [13] Lastly, the operator should round all line and point angles. Occlusal-lingual and occlusal-buccal angles are beveled at 45 degrees. Bur is held parallel to the tooth’s long axis, and one should carefully blend the surfaces. Remove any remaining caries and perform appropriate pulp therapy if pulp involvement is present. The rubber dam should be left in place until cementing the crown. If it interferes with the seating and adaptation of the crown, it can be removed after tooth preparation. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Occlusal Reduction
retrieved_docs.content: Operators can use 69L or 169L bur to reduce 1.5 to 2.0 mm of the occlusal surface. The cusps original contour needs to be preserved. Occlusal depth guidance grooves could be given for better reduction. If a sufficient amount of occlusal surface has already been lost due to caries, marginal ridges of neighboring teeth can be used as a reference. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Bruxism Management -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment for bruxism focuses on preventing additional tooth damage and relieving associated symptoms. The effectiveness of available treatments varies considerably. [17] Although orthodontic interventions are a potential option due to the proposed association between bruxism and malocclusion, this idea lacks consensus among dental professionals and supporting evidence from clinical studies. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Restoration of Bruxism/Hypoplastic Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: Greater occlusal wear results from bruxism and hypoplastic disease, causing decreased vertical height. In this case, occlusion can be improved by adding a solder layer from the crown's impression surface to prevent or reduce occlusal reduction (Croll technique). The rest of the tooth preparation and crown adaptation is standard. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Sleep Study -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Evaluation of response to positive airway pressure (PAP), oral appliance, or positional therapy

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Teeth -- Indications -- Orthodontic Extractions
retrieved_docs.content: Teeth are often considered for extraction for orthodontic reasons, often to create space in the dental arch to allow movement of the teeth into a more favorable position. [21] A plan for which teeth are to be removed should be formulated by an orthodontist.

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Procedure
retrieved_docs.content: Place orthodontic separators between the contact point of the primary molar for 4 or 5 days to create room for the crown. Select the smallest-sized crown covering all the cusps, approaching the contact points, and giving a 'spring back' effect. Adjustments of the crown are to be made using band-forming pliers if necessary. Cementation of the crown with glass ionomer cement. The tooth must be maintained in occlusion until the cement sets, followed by excess removal using floss. [25] [26] The Hall technique should be chosen as an alternative when the traditional approach with crown preparation is implausible. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Cone Beam Computed Tomography -- Issues of Concern -- Cone Beam Computed Tomography General Recommendations
retrieved_docs.content: Imaging with cone beam computed tomography before implant surgery is more useful than post-implant imaging.

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Undersized Crown
retrieved_docs.content: The crown wall of the best-fitting crown can be cut buccally or lingually, and an additional piece of 0.004-inch orthodontic stainless steel band can be welded over the gap that is then contoured to the desired form.

retrieved_docs.title: Le Fort Osteotomy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Fabricated surgical splints should be fitted to the dentition. The maxilla should be manipulated into position, and the patient should be placed into maxillomandibular fixation utilizing stainless steel wires. The entire maxillomandibular complex should be rotated, ensuring the mandibular condyles are seated in their respective fossae. Failure to seat the condyles may result in postoperative malocclusion.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Occlusal Contact Relations And Mandibular Movements -- Structure and Function -- Dental Occlusal Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: Tooth cusps function to guide erupting teeth into appropriate occlusion with periodontal stimulation informing neural reflexes for jaw closure and mastication [1] . The maxillary palatal cusps and the mandibular facial cusps comprise the centric cusps involved in centric occlusion (discussed below). In contrast, the maxillary buccal cusp and the mandibular lingual cusps guide the mandible during the lateral excursion.

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Crown Adaptation
retrieved_docs.content: Place the crown linguo-buccally on the prepared tooth, applying pressure in the buccal direction so the crown slips over the buccal surface and into the gingival sulcus. There is friction as the crown slides over the buccal bulge. Crown positioning be difficult if the crown is too small or if the buccal bulge is too excessive. Choose a larger crown or reduce the tooth's buccal bulge in this case. After placing the selected crown over the prepared tooth, ask the patient to bite over it. Check for preliminary occlusal and marginal ridge relation. Previously, scratch a mark on the stainless steel crown at the level of the free gingival margin. Scratch or dotted lines indicate the gingival contour and the portion of the crown to be removed. Remove the crown and trim or cut away the additional crown portion gingivally 1 mm below the scratch line with a crown and bridge scissors or, preferably, a large abrasive wheel (“heatless stone”) that allows for more precise reduction. Repeat the procedure until the adequate extension of 1 mm beneath the gingival margin is achieved.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Crown lengthening may not be possible if restorative margins extend into furcations or result in excessive bone removal. Crown exposure is not indicated in excessive gingival display caused by hyperactive lip muscles, skeletal maxillary excess, or a short upper lip.

retrieved_docs.title: Posterior Crossbite -- Treatment / Management -- Management of Posterior Crossbite
retrieved_docs.content: NiTi Expanders: These nickel-titanium wire shapes can be attached to a lingual sheath welded to molar bands cemented to the maxillary first permanent molars. Various sizes are available and must be selected depending on the amount of expansion desired and the pretreatment width of the palate.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2587
retrieved_docs.content: Bismuth subnitrate and bismuth subcitrate are therapeutic agents that may be used to treat الإِسْهال (diarrhea) and اضطراب مَعِدِيّ (gastrointestinal disturbance). Both compounds share the same clinical indications, offering similar benefits for managing these gastrointestinal conditions. However, each is contraindicated in the presence of الاعتلال الخثري (renal impairment), meaning that patients with renal disease should not receive either bismuth subnitrate or bismuth subcitrate. Recognizing the parallel efficacy and shared contraindication of these bismuth salts is essential for safe prescribing and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1989
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids for local oral treatment and other agents for local oral treatment in ATC are both classified as stomatological preparations, indicating that each is a type of medication formulated for use within the oral cavity to manage dental or mucosal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1326
retrieved_docs.content: Bismuth subnitrate and bismuth subcitrate are both indicated for the treatment of gastrointestinal disturbances (اضْطِراب مَعِدِيّ). retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.
